*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Grace_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Mount%20Grace%20Priory/@54.3833,-1.2833,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZOlMoqPf0FvVcAWK3Mkcb0pgYMrNE5gNJ_ADPs6q1oCipOdhlUV7pszzgtzv9rWHVjVK4HShX5mYeaZJboVht-9ebIC9lOHp3sU1TsKGKUjCcRJsCEnBBXzRmxylq22pCsRTEsGxOJXSWfh=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Mount Grace Priory is a monastery in the parish of East Harlsey, North Yorkshire, England. Set in woodlands within the North York Moors National Park, it is represented today by the best preserved and most accessible ruins among the nine houses of the Carthusian Order, which existed in England in the Middle Ages and were known as charterhouses.

_Listing 2 of 50_

listing.desc: Mount Grace Priory is a monastery in the parish of East Harlsey, North Yorkshire, England. imageAttribution.description: The best preserved Carthusian Priory in Britain.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Rippon Cathedral
*Country:* england
*Region:* yorkshire
*County:* Yorkshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 7th century / 12th-16th century
*Coords:* 54.13472222, -1.52
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ripon_Cathedral
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Rippon%20Cathedral/@54.1381,-1.5217,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Ripon_Cathedral_Exterior%2C_Nth_Yorkshire%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/330px-Ripon_Cathedral_Exterior%2C_Nth_Yorkshire%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://riponcathedral.org.uk/

*Description:*
The Cathedral Church of St Peter and St Wilfrid, commonly known as Ripon Cathedral, and until 1836 known as Ripon Minster, is a cathedral in Ripon, North Yorkshire, England. Founded as a monastery by monks of the Irish tradition in the 660s, it was refounded as a Benedictine monastery by St Wilfrid in 672. The church became collegiate in the tenth century, and acted as a mother church within the large Diocese of York for the remainder of the Middle Ages. The present church is the fourth, and was built between the 13th and 16th centuries. In 1836 the church became the cathedral for the Diocese of Ripon. In 2014 the Diocese was incorporated into the new Diocese of Leeds, and the church became one of three co-equal cathedrals of the Bishop of Leeds.

_Listing 3 of 50_

listing.name: Rippon Cathedral
listing.desc: The Cathedral Church of St Peter and St Wilfrid, commonly known as Ripon Cathedral, and until 1836 known as Ripon Minster, is a cathedral in Ripon, North Yorkshire, England. Founded as a monastery by monks of the Irish tradition in the 660s, it was refounded as a Benedictine monastery by St Wilfrid in 672. The church became collegiate in the tenth century, and acted as a mother church within the large Diocese of York for the remainder of the Middle Ages. The present church is the fourth, and was built between the 13th and 16th centuries. In 1836 the church became the cathedral for the Diocese of Ripon. In 2014 the Diocese was incorporated into the new Diocese of Leeds, and the church became one of three co-equal cathedrals of the Bishop of Leeds.

imageAttribution.description: Ripon Cathedral's western facade as viewed from Minster Rd in North Yorkshire, England.

*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNI8yAIRwtMIsjGf1Hwb_LKvMv7mdpEspVLA4o4jyruc1dw4PkgyV4CZ-ec-ztCAOKmBueShmhkDotqtNuUqrtZyVUv7cdaLqruwwYrIGFyGeyN-sydeit1_tjfH21B2zU_Z4-wH759n9dBuJY=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/KqCDQkgK3R1xfe3S7


*Description:*
Brodsworth Hall, near Brodsworth, 5 miles (8 km) north-west of Doncaster in South Yorkshire, is one of the most complete surviving examples of a Victorian country house in England. It is virtually unchanged since the 1860s. It was designed in the Italianate style by the obscure London architect, Philip Wilkinson, then 26 years old. He was commissioned by Charles Sabine Augustus Thellusson, who inherited the estate in 1859, but the original estate was constructed in 1791 for merchant and slave owner Peter Thellusson. It is a Grade I listed building.

_Listing 4 of 50_

It is a Grade I listed building.

*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Nostell Priory is a Palladian house in Nostell, West Yorkshire, in England, near Crofton and on the road to Doncaster from Wakefield. It dates from 1733 and was built for the Winn family on the site of a medieval priory. The Priory and its contents were given to the National Trust in 1953 by the trustees of the estate and Rowland Winn, 3rd Baron St Oswald.

_Listing 5 of 50_

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Bryn Celli Ddu
*Country:* wales
*Region:* snowdonia north
*County:* Anglesey
*Type:* monument
*Period:* Neolithic
*Coords:* 53.2077, -4.2361
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bryn_Celli_Ddu
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Bryn%20Celli%20Ddu/@53.2072,-4.2361,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPiVWPA6MKBzVoHlcp23V1CmirXCEgUKOlcyUjAHRCqDXf2ZK4Qc_p5h6KhNqW1BPr3p-h3KL3fF0Y60CcZhF3QKWN8I_t6gIBlVzdEjjj0QQWMaVr4jksXyQ-F1OzgnVB-vrREg0EyPy2WVyE2okg4cA=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/2Nuu6V2kWzsY5GjL7

*Description:*
Bryn Celli Ddu is a prehistoric site on the Welsh island of Anglesey located near Llanddaniel Fab. Its name means 'the mound in the dark grove'. It was archaeologically excavated between 1928 and 1929. Visitors can get inside the mound through a stone passage to the burial chamber, and it is the centrepiece of a major Neolithic Scheduled Monument in the care of Cadw. The presence of a mysterious pillar within the burial chamber, the reproduction of the 'Pattern Stone', carved with sinuous serpentine designs, and the fact that the site was once a henge with a stone circle, and may have been used to plot the date of the summer solstice have all attracted much interest.

_Listing 6 of 50_

Visitors can get inside the mound through a stone passage to the burial chamber, and it is the centrepiece of a major Neolithic Scheduled Monument in the care of Cadw. The presence of a mysterious pillar within the burial chamber, the reproduction of the 'Pattern Stone', carved with sinuous serpentine designs, and the fact that the site was once a henge with a stone circle, and may have been used to plot the date of the summer solstice have all attracted much interest.

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Tredegar House
*Country:* wales
*Region:* south wales
*County:* Newport
*Type:* historic-house
*Period:* 17th century
*Coords:* 51.56157222, -3.02916667
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tredegar_House
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Tredegar%20House/@51.5597,-3.0167,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZN8U_w-VGHIKzyyPaBLpnKuUXRXcJK38fGzOZFqo9cO3NM70mjV62Rgi4DcwmmJuH8g7A6arndmnY2-6XcDzGTqeZ-4NSQo8tDSimZVRx41VM4nj7k0JE-k8GD-yUoXPfrvjGMaMEHmjcYqhg=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/K9mWKJF9JF2GXejeA

*Description:*
Tredegar House is a 17th-century Charles II-era country house in Coedkernew, on the southwestern edge of Newport, Wales. For over five hundred years it was home to the Morgan family, later Lords Tredegar, one of the most powerful and influential families in the area. Described as "the grandest and most exuberant country house in Monmouthshire" and one of the "outstanding houses of the Restoration period in the whole of Britain", the mansion stands in a reduced landscaped garden of 90 acres (0.36 km2). The property became a Grade I listed building on 3 March 1952 and has been under the care of the National Trust since March 2012. The park surrounding the house is designated Grade II* on the Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales.

_Listing 7 of 50_

listing.name: Tredegar House
listing.desc: Tredegar House is a 17th-century Charles II-era country house in Coedkernew, on the southwestern edge of Newport, Wales. For over five hundred years it was home to the Morgan family, later Lords Tredegar, one of the most powerful and influential families in the area. Described as "the grandest and most exuberant country house in Monmouthshire" and one of the "outstanding houses of the Restoration period in the whole of Britain", the mansion stands in a reduced landscaped garden of 90 acres (0.36 km2). The property became a Grade I listed building on 3 March 1952 and has been under the care of the National Trust since March 2012. The park surrounding the house is designated Grade II* on the Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales.

*Country:* ireland
*Region:* irelands ancient east
*County:* County Meath
*Type:* castle
*Period:* Neolithic-Medieval
*Coords:* 53.5775, -6.61194444
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hill_of_Tara
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Hill%20of%20Tara/@53.578,-6.612,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Stone_of_Destiny_2018-07-24.jpg/330px-Stone_of_Destiny_2018-07-24.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/cnNHNkiTm6FqKf8h9


*Description:*
The Hill of Tara is a hill and ancient ceremonial and burial site near Skryne in County Meath, Ireland. Tradition identifies the hill as the inauguration place and seat of the High Kings of Ireland; it also appears in Irish mythology. Tara consists of numerous monuments and earthworks—dating from the Neolithic to the Iron Age—including a passage tomb, burial mounds, round enclosures, a standing stone, and a ceremonial avenue. There is also a church and graveyard on the hill. Tara forms part of a larger ancient landscape and Tara itself is a protected national monument under the care of the Office of Public Works, an agency of the Irish Government.

_Listing 8 of 50_

listing.name: Hill of Tara
listing.desc: The Hill of Tara is a hill and ancient ceremonial and burial site near Skryne in County Meath, Ireland. Tradition identifies the hill as the inauguration place and seat of the High Kings of Ireland; it also appears in Irish mythology. Tara consists of numerous monuments and earthworks—dating from the Neolithic to the Iron Age—including a passage tomb, burial mounds, round enclosures, a standing stone, and a ceremonial avenue. There is also a church and graveyard on the hill. Tara forms part of a larger ancient landscape and Tara itself is a protected national monument under the care of the Office of Public Works, an agency of the Irish Government.

imageAttribution.description: The Stone of Destiny (Lia Fáil) at the Hill of Tara, once used as a coronation stone for the High Kings of Ireland.

*Description:*
Blarney Castle is a medieval stronghold in Blarney, a town in Cork, Ireland. Though earlier fortifications were built on the same spot, the current keep was built by the MacCarthy of Muskerry dynasty, a cadet branch of the Kings of Desmond, and dates from 1446. The Blarney Stone is among the machicolations of the castle.

_Listing 9 of 50_

*Description:*
Florence Court is a large 18th-century house and estate located eight miles (13 km) south-west of Enniskillen, County Fermanagh, Northern Ireland. It is set in the foothills of Cuilcagh Mountain. The nearby village is distinguished by the one-word name Florencecourt. It is owned and managed by the National Trust and is the sister property of nearby Castle Coole. The other National Trust property in County Fermanagh is the Crom Estate.

_Listing 10 of 50_

Kilmacduagh Monastery is a ruined abbey near the town of Gort in County Galway, Ireland. It was the birthplace of the Diocese of Kilmacduagh. It was reportedly founded by Saint Colman, son of Duagh in the 7th century, on land given him by his cousin King Guaire Aidne mac Colmáin of Connacht.

*Name:* Hulne Priory
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Northumberland
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 13th-16th century
*Coords:* 55.43492, -1.74395
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hulne_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Hulne%20Priory/@55.415,-1.7567,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Hulne_Priory.jpg/330px-Hulne_Priory.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* needs more research to build out a page. 

*Description:*
Hulne Priory, Hulne Friary or Hulne Abbey was a friary founded in 1240 by the Carmelites or 'Whitefriars'. It is said that the Northumberland site, quite close to Alnwick, was chosen for some slight resemblance to Mount Carmel where the order originated. Substantial ruins survive, watched over by the stone figures of friars carved in the 18th century. It is a sign of the unrest felt in this area so near to the border with Scotland that the priory had a surrounding wall and in the 15th century a pele tower was erected. Changes were made at the Dissolution of the Monasteries when the Percy family took control.

_Listing 15 of 50_

listing.desc: Hulne Priory, Hulne Friary or Hulne Abbey was a friary founded in 1240 by the Carmelites or 'Whitefriars'. It is said that the Northumberland site, quite close to Alnwick, was chosen for some slight resemblance to Mount Carmel where the order originated. Substantial ruins survive, watched over by the stone figures of friars carved in the 18th century. It is a sign of the unrest felt in this area so near to the border with Scotland that the priory had a surrounding wall and in the 15th century a pele tower was erected. Changes were made at the Dissolution of the Monasteries when the Percy family took control.

The Castle, Newcastle, or Newcastle Castle is a medieval fortification in Newcastle upon Tyne, England, built on the site of the fortress that gave the City of Newcastle its name. The most prominent remaining structures on the site are the Castle Keep, and the Black Gate, its fortified gatehouse.

*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNqp434tQPR7euTgBR_NCbt-EASL3LocbK40k6kGMSx9hIKCNTQ8DJSgIDV3wy5aIwqEDr1IKacbjETZPCbqW41cQQpq3h_oh1-zNPlOOokn9zxxnYfdizugCGkYrmUTiJmKHPaZBqVDDZohGo=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Rufford Old Hall is a National Trust property in Rufford, Lancashire, in north-west England. Built in the late fifteenth or early sixteenth centuries for the Hesketh family, only the great hall survives from the original structure. A brick-built wing in the Jacobean style was added in 1661, at right angles to the great hall, and a third wing was added in the 1820s.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Walmer Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* Kent
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 16th century
*Coords:* 51.200992, 1.402308
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walmer_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Walmer%20Castle/@51.1969,1.4005,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Walmer_Castle_from_the_west.jpg/330px-Walmer_Castle_from_the_west.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/UXN6eUPoE4cYi3iAA


*Description:*
Walmer Castle is an artillery fort originally constructed by Henry VIII in Walmer, Kent, between 1539 and 1540. It formed part of the King's Device programme to protect against invasion from France and the Holy Roman Empire, and defended the strategically important Downs anchorage off the English coast. Comprising a keep and four circular bastions, the moated stone castle covered 0.61 acres (0.25 ha) and had 39 firing positions on the upper levels for artillery. It cost the Crown a total of £27,092 to build the three castles of Walmer, Sandown, and Deal, which lay adjacent to one another along the coast and were connected by earthwork defences. The original invasion threat passed, but during the Second English Civil War of 1648–49, Walmer was seized by pro-Royalist insurgents and was only retaken by Parliamentary forces after several months' fighting.

_Listing 18 of 50_

It formed part of the King's Device programme to protect against invasion from France and the Holy Roman Empire, and defended the strategically important Downs anchorage off the English coast. Comprising a keep and four circular bastions, the moated stone castle covered 0.61 acres (0.25 ha) and had 39 firing positions on the upper levels for artillery. It cost the Crown a total of £27,092 to build the three castles of Walmer, Sandown, and Deal, which lay adjacent to one another along the coast and were connected by earthwork defences. The original invasion threat passed, but during the Second English Civil War of 1648–49, Walmer was seized by pro-Royalist insurgents and was only retaken by Parliamentary forces after several months' fighting.

*County:* Hampshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 50.87888889, -1.3575
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netley_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Netley%20Abbey/@50.8806,-1.3592,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Netley_abbey_drone.JPG/330px-Netley_abbey_drone.JPG
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Netley Abbey is a ruined late medieval monastery in the village of Netley near Southampton in Hampshire, England. The abbey was founded in 1239 as a house for monks of the austere Cistercian order. Despite royal patronage, Netley was never rich, produced no influential scholars nor churchmen, and its nearly 300-year history was quiet. The monks were best known to their neighbours for the generous hospitality they offered to travellers on land and sea.

_Listing 19 of 50_

Avebury Manor & Garden is a National Trust property consisting of a Grade I listed early-16th-century manor house and its surrounding garden. It is in Avebury, near Marlborough, Wiltshire, England, in the centre of the village next to St James's Church and close to the Avebury neolithic henge monument.
